definition_population.content: SPENCDI (OMIM 607944) is a rare immune-skeletal dysplasia with spondylar/metaphyseal lesions, immune dysfunction, and neurological involvement, caused by biallelic ACP5 mutation; autosomal recessive, presents in childhood.

sources.quote: an uncommon immune-skeletal dysplasia with heterogeneous manifestations and variable severity
a rare autosomal recessive genetic disorder caused by a homozygous mutation of the ACP5 gene

epidemiology.content: Not established in this pass — only case-report/case-series literature (n=3-4 patients) found; no population-based prevalence/incidence estimate identified.

biology.content: Disease is caused by biallelic ACP5 loss-of-function, producing a type I interferonopathy with autoimmune cytopenias and lupus-like features. No PubMed literature found linking the platform's ZNF627 target to this disease.

sources.quote: a potential diagnosis of spondyloenchondrodysplasia (SPENCD), which was confirmed with the identification of biallelic ACP5 mutations
Large spectrum of immunologic abnormalities may be seen in SPENCD, including immune deficiencies and autoimmune disorders

treatment.content: Not established in this pass — no FDA-approved disease-specific therapy identified; case reports describe supportive/immunosuppressive management of specific manifestations only.

regulatory_trials.content: Zero interventional trials found on ClinicalTrials.gov for spondyloenchondrodysplasia in this pass.

sources.quote: count_total query for condition=spondyloenchondrodysplasia, study_type=INTERVENTIONAL returned total=0

gaps: No PubMed hit for 'ZNF627 spondyloenchondrodysplasia' — the platform's high genetic-association score (0.94) for this gene-disease pair could not be corroborated with an independent published mechanism or GWAS finding in this pass.
No epidemiology, treatment, or trial data found — this is an ultra-rare monogenic disease with a very thin evidence base overall.

Established disease-causing gene is ACP5, not ZNF627; the relationship between the platform's Perturb-seq CD4+ T-cell signal for ZNF627 and this ultra-rare monogenic disease is unclear from literature alone.
